- base-query: 通过 Obsidian CLI 只读查询笔记的操作手册：给定一个 `.base` 文件，返回满足其过滤条件的笔记路径清单。所有查询 base 的需求都由 `dp-base` subagent 承接，主对话不直接触发。subagent 启动时预载本手册，按手册执行 `obsidian base:query` 并把路径清单回传上游。
- mapping-ingest: 把源料页面吸收进认知地图（mapping）。
- base-author: 编写和修改 Obsidian Base 文件（`.base`）的操作手册。创建 base、调整过滤器和公式、修改列与分组排序，这些工作都由 `dp-base` subagent 承接，主对话不直接触发。subagent 启动时预载本手册，按四轴设计框架与 Step 1-4 骨架执行。filter、formula、view 的语法参考见 `references/`。
- dp-search: 用 Obsidian 全文搜索的查询语言（boolean / scope / property / 比较 / 正则 / 大小写）做进阶检索。最常见的触发是 LLM 在 moving 阶段做范围检索需要操作符组合：限定到某子树、按 frontmatter 属性筛、找未链接提及 (unlinked mention)、按行 / 块 / 段邻近度匹配、按 task 状态筛、用 regex 抓结构模式。使用场景：（1）单关键词 `obsidian search:context` 命中过多 / 过少需要操作符细化；（2）需要按 frontmatter 属性 / tag / 任务状态 / 路径范围筛；（3）需要按行 / 块 / 段的邻近度匹配；（4）需要 regex 抓结构（wikilink 模式、块 id、日期）。**简单单关键词搜索仍走 raw `obsidian search:context query="<text>" path=...`，不必经本 skill**。
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the current diff and apply the fixes — equivalent to /code-review --fix.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	---
2	aliases:
3	  - 心智动作阶段性形成
4	  - 心智动作的阶段性形成理论
5	  - поэтапное формирование умственных действий
6	---
7	
8	[[mapping/entities/piotr-galperin.md|Galperin]] 提出的理论框架，核心主张是物质性动作（material action）可以经由一系列阶段转化为心智动作（mental action），而动作的质量取决于其「定向部分」（orientation part）的完备程度。定向部分包括对情境的表征、行动计划的拟定、执行过程中的控制与纠正；另一部分是执行本身，虽依赖定向但不等同于定向（[[Library/Towards Research of the Intellectual Development of the Child.md|Galperin，1968]]）。
9	
10	## 定向基础与正确执行条件
11	
12	理论用两个概念刻画学习过程的核心变量。定向基础（Orienting Basis of Action，Oa）指个体在执行动作时实际依据的全部条件；正确执行条件系统（System of Conditions for Correct Performance，Sp）指要让新动作「从第一次起就正确、此后每次都正确」所需考虑的全部条件。两者的关系决定学习类型：Oa < Sp 为第一类型，Oa = Sp 为第二类型。如果 Oa 包含多余或错误的标记（Oa > Sp），功能上等同于 Oa < Sp。
13	
14	## 三种学习类型
15	
16	第一类型（Oa < Sp）：个体缺少正确执行新动作所必需的部分标记，只能通过盲目尝试弥补缺口。错误不可避免，正确动作的建立缓慢，过程分为「确立大致结构」和「逐步完善」两个时长悬殊的阶段。即使最终达到高精度，由于客观条件的相当部分仍未被个体认识，动作对条件变化极为敏感，个体间差异大，迁移能力差。
17	
18	第二类型（Oa = Sp）：个体获得执行新任务所需的完整标记系统，省去第一类型中最漫长的试错阶段。每个动作环节都与其客观条件对应，动作既成功又具有一定的理据性。个体间成绩趋于均匀，迁移能力明显优于第一类型，但仍不完整。根本局限在于每个新任务的完整 Oa 都需要从头提供，因为迁移始终不够充分。
19	
20	第三类型：不再逐一提供完整 Oa，而是教给个体一种分析方法，使其能自行为特定领域内的任何新现象构造完整的 Oa。这种分析方法依赖两个要素：该领域材料的「基本单元」（basic units）和这些单元在具体现象中的「组合规则」（general rules of combination）。个体面对新对象时，用这两种方法分析出完整且理性的 Oa，以此为基础习得相关动作和概念。
21	
22	## 第三类型的实验证据
23	
24	第三类型学习在书写、语法和算术三个领域得到实验验证。
25	
26	在书写教学中，以「不变方向线段」为轮廓的基本单元，6 岁儿童学会分析和再现俄语字母后，能自主掌握全部 58 个字母（含大写），并将分析能力迁移到拉丁字母、亚美尼亚文、阿拉伯文、速记符号乃至平面轨迹描绘。
27	
28	在语法教学中，以语义素（seme，最小信息单元）取代词素（morpheme）作为词的基本单元。儿童通过系统变换词的各部分来发现各语义素，分析不同词类后形成通用的词结构图式。语言变成主动运用不同语义素的活动过程，而非被动记忆语法规则。
29	
30	在算术教学中，以测量作为数的来源，「单位」被定义为「被测量且等于其自身度量的东西」。L.S. Georgiyev 用此方法在幼儿园对 50 名 6 岁儿童进行了 68 节课（每节 25 至 30 分钟）的集体教学。教学前后分别施测 16 道 Piaget 问题：传统教学的对照组（60 人）在各题上正确率为 8% 至 78%，实验组教学前正确率为 4% 至 52%，教学后正确率为 92% 至 100%。实验组儿童不再表现出 Piaget 所描述的数量守恒缺失现象，而这一能力并非教学直接教授的内容。
31	
32	## 学习与思维发展
33	
34	第三类型学习带来三项根本性的认知转变。第一，事物从「整体即价值」变为「多个相对独立参数的集合」：儿童此前把事物视为不可分的整体，任一显眼属性的变化等同于整个事物的变化；此后则区分各个参数，比较时只针对被问及的那个属性。第二，事物的每个属性从直觉印象变为「基本单元的多重集合」（multiplicity），属性被度量分解为等价元素后成为可操作的数学对象。第三，这些多重集合获得统一的内部组织，即自然数的生成规则（n +/- 1）和十进制排列。「数量守恒原则」在此基础上形成：如果既未增加也未减少任何东西，事物在某一参数上的变化不涉及其他参数的变化。
35	
36	这三项变化揭示了学习的全部成果可以分为两个不等的部分：事物的一般图式（general patterns）决定看待和思考事物的新方式，具体事实和规律则构成所学领域的科学内容。后者在体量上远大于前者，但对思维发展的重要性远不如前者。只有第三类型学习才让一般图式与科学知识的关系变得清晰：教学明确包含了一般化图式的形成，这些图式经由学习成为个体动作的一般化统一图式，进而成为思维的新结构。在其他类型学习中，这些一般图式隐含在所学知识之中，个体对它们没有意识，实践只能产生自发的、直觉式的接近，且因人而异。这正是 [[mapping/entities/lev-vygotsky.md|Vygotsky]]（学习推动发展）与 [[mapping/entities/jean-piaget.md|Piaget]]（发展自发进行，学习只能在已达到的发展水平上有效）之间分歧的根源：在第三类型学习出现之前，知识习得与思维发展之间的关系过于遥远和模糊，允许截然不同的解释。
37

[[mapping/entities/piotr-galperin.md|Galperin]] 提出的理论框架，核心主张是物质性动作（material action）可以经由一系列阶段转化为心智动作（mental action），而动作的质量取决于其「定向部分」（orientation part）的完备程度。定向部分包括对情境的表征、行动计划的拟定、执行过程中的控制与纠正；另一部分是执行本身，虽依赖定向但不等同于定向（[[Library/Towards Research of the Intellectual Development of the Child.md|Galperin，1968]]）。

## 定向基础与正确执行条件

理论用两个概念刻画学习过程的核心变量。定向基础（Orienting Basis of Action，Oa）指个体在执行动作时实际依据的全部条件；正确执行条件系统（System of Conditions for Correct Performance，Sp）指要让新动作「从第一次起就正确、此后每次都正确」所需考虑的全部条件。两者的关系决定学习类型：Oa < Sp 为第一类型，Oa = Sp 为第二类型。如果 Oa 包含多余或错误的标记（Oa > Sp），功能上等同于 Oa < Sp。

## 三种学习类型

第一类型（Oa < Sp）：个体缺少正确执行新动作所必需的部分标记，只能通过盲目尝试弥补缺口。错误不可避免，正确动作的建立缓慢，过程分为「确立大致结构」和「逐步完善」两个时长悬殊的阶段。即使最终达到高精度，由于客观条件的相当部分仍未被个体认识，动作对条件变化极为敏感，个体间差异大，迁移能力差。

第二类型（Oa = Sp）：个体获得执行新任务所需的完整标记系统，省去第一类型中最漫长的试错阶段。每个动作环节都与其客观条件对应，动作既成功又具有一定的理据性。个体间成绩趋于均匀，迁移能力明显优于第一类型，但仍不完整。根本局限在于每个新任务的完整 Oa 都需要从头提供，因为迁移始终不够充分。

第三类型：不再逐一提供完整 Oa，而是教给个体一种分析方法，使其能自行为特定领域内的任何新现象构造完整的 Oa。这种分析方法依赖两个要素：该领域材料的「基本单元」（basic units）和这些单元在具体现象中的「组合规则」（general rules of combination）。个体面对新对象时，用这两种方法分析出完整且理性的 Oa，以此为基础习得相关动作和概念。

## 第三类型的实验证据

第三类型学习在书写、语法和算术三个领域得到实验验证。

在书写教学中，以「不变方向线段」为轮廓的基本单元，6 岁儿童学会分析和再现俄语字母后，能自主掌握全部 58 个字母（含大写），并将分析能力迁移到拉丁字母、亚美尼亚文、阿拉伯文、速记符号乃至平面轨迹描绘。

在语法教学中，以语义素（seme，最小信息单元）取代词素（morpheme）作为词的基本单元。儿童通过系统变换词的各部分来发现各语义素，分析不同词类后形成通用的词结构图式。语言变成主动运用不同语义素的活动过程，而非被动记忆语法规则。

在算术教学中，以测量作为数的来源，「单位」被定义为「被测量且等于其自身度量的东西」。L.S. Georgiyev 用此方法在幼儿园对 50 名 6 岁儿童进行了 68 节课（每节 25 至 30 分钟）的集体教学。教学前后分别施测 16 道 Piaget 问题：传统教学的对照组（60 人）在各题上正确率为 8% 至 78%，实验组教学前正确率为 4% 至 52%，教学后正确率为 92% 至 100%。实验组儿童不再表现出 Piaget 所描述的数量守恒缺失现象，而这一能力并非教学直接教授的内容。

## 学习与思维发展

第三类型学习带来三项根本性的认知转变。第一，事物从「整体即价值」变为「多个相对独立参数的集合」：儿童此前把事物视为不可分的整体，任一显眼属性的变化等同于整个事物的变化；此后则区分各个参数，比较时只针对被问及的那个属性。第二，事物的每个属性从直觉印象变为「基本单元的多重集合」（multiplicity），属性被度量分解为等价元素后成为可操作的数学对象。第三，这些多重集合获得统一的内部组织，即自然数的生成规则（n +/- 1）和十进制排列。「数量守恒原则」在此基础上形成：如果既未增加也未减少任何东西，事物在某一参数上的变化不涉及其他参数的变化。

26	## TOWARDS RESEARCH OF THE INTELLECTUAL DEVELOPMENT OF THE CHILD
27	
28	PETER I. GALPERIN
29	*University of Moscow, Moscow, USSR*
30	
31	La méthode de formation par étapes des activités et des concepts mentaux a permis de distinguer trois principaux types dans les méthodes d'enseignement. Chacun d'eux se caractérise par la manière particulière dont s'oriente l'enfant dans la matière étudiée et dont se présente cette matière en fonction de l'orientation choisie. Lorsque l'enseignement dans une matière est organisé selon les exigences du IIIe type de méthode, on voit pour la première fois clairement apparaître et se différencier — ( 1 ) les schémas généraux des objets étudiés et — ( 2 ) les connaissances concrètes particulières (y compris les lois). Seuls les schémas généraux sont en rapport direct avec le développement de la pensée : ils dessinent à la fois la structuration générale de la matière étudiée et les lignes générales de l'orientation dans cette matière. Une fois assimilés, ces schèmes deviennent les structures générales de la pensée. Ce sont ces schèmes, et eux seuls, qui constituent l'élément de liaison entre l'apprentissage et le développement de la pensée. Avec les autres méthodes pédagogiques, ces schèmes restent cachés, l'enfant n'est pas conscient de les approcher et l'on manque de faits précis relativement à la liaison entre apprentissage et développement, qui est ainsi l'objet d'interprétations très différentes. C'est pourquoi, à l'heure actuelle, il n'est pas de meilleure manière d'aborder l'étude, non pas statique (comme le font les "coupes transversales"), mais dynamique du développement de la pensée que de faire apprendre à l'enfant les activités mentales et les concepts en l'orientant dans les matières à étudier comme le propose la méthode de type III.
32	
33	The study of the intellectual development of the child was hitherto limited to cross-section methods that allowed only to show the level of development achieved. We did not know how concepts are formed in the child; we just gave him tasks requiring the formation of a concept and then found out what he could perform. The method of determining the "zone of proximal development" by the difference in the results between independent action and joint child-and-adult activity merely complicated the scheme, without removing this basic limitation. By building up a serial row of such static indexes, we traced the curve of intellectual development, while its dynamic forces and its very necessity remained concealed. Taking into account various circumstances, one could have very different conceptions about these things. Such conceptions were in fact suggested, for example, by Vygotsky ( 1935 ) and Piaget ( 1953 ). Vygotsky said that "learning pushes ahead leading development in its wake", while Piaget asserts that intellectual development takes place spontaneously and that an efficient learning is possible only on the basis and in the measure of the level of development reached. The possibility of such a difference shows up clearly that the problem of method is the very first problem in studying the intellectual development of the child. In this perspective, we have better start with the description of how our method (Galperin, 1959 ) was formed and how it brought us one day to face the problem of mental development of the child, a subject we had been dodging stubbornly all the time.
34	
35	## THE METHOD OF FORMATION OF MENTAL ACTIONS
36	
37	Our method is more commonly known as "stage-by-stage formation of mental actions". Why "mental actions"? Let us compare two extreme situations: the initial one, when the child can carry out a new action (addition, sound analysis of a word, or, vice versa, the integration of word sound, etc.) only supported by external objects and external manipulation of these objects, and the final one, when the same action is performed by the child, mentally this time and, as it were, automatically, but with understanding. The former is material action; the latter is, we would say, not at all an action, even if only imagined, but rather an idea of the action, in which the initial sensorial content of action becomes a remote addressee, while the idea itself appears to be something purely psychic. But in this case, the objective action and the idea of this action constitute the two extreme links of an whole process and, in their genetic succession, show something like transformation of a material process into a psychic process. It seems, at last, that we can catch a glimpse to the mysterious birth, not of the psychics in general, but of a concrete psychic process, and, consequently, join together and explain what had hitherto remained hopelessly separated and incomprehensible: the actual content of psychic "acts", their "appearance" in self-observation, the functions of this phenomenon and its proper mechanisms. Of course, this is only an hypothesis, but such an hypothesis was worth trying out! It has become a starting point in our study of mental actions or, to be more exact, of their formation.
38	
39	At the beginning of this study, we felt too helpless to conceive models of a still unknown process and decided, instead, to hold to facts of reality of teaching various mental actions at school. There, as in life, actions are performed not for the sake of performance, but with the aim of obtaining a definite result. According to the degree of success with which the action is performed under various conditions, the child is given marks, *i.e.*, his ability is graduated. Thus two important points are accepted *de facto*: first one, the action performed by the child may possess different properties and, second one, the task of teaching is to bring forth actions having definite, pre-determined properties. It seems that differences found for a same action in different children are caused by different comprehension of this action and different ability of performing it in various circumstances. "Comprehension" and "ability" are subjective denominations given to the two basic parts of objective actions as performed by the child. One of these parts which is briefly referred to as "comprehension" has been called "part of orientation" because of its objective role in action. It includes the elaboration of a representation of circumstances, the outline of a plan of action, the control and correction during the performance. The second part of the objective action is the performance itself which, though it depends on the orientation, cannot be identified with it.
40	
41	The part of orientation is the monitoring element; it fundamentally determines the quality of the performance. If we draw up a list of situations in which the application of this action is required by the plan of teaching, this list will show the whole of the requirements which the action is to comply with and, at the same time, the whole of the properties in keeping with these requirements that we wish to form. Thus, the task is not merely to form the action, but to have it formed with definite pre-conditioned properties. Such a task decisively changes the whole strategy of research: instead of studying how the action is being formed, we now have to cope with a new task of finding out and, if necessary, creating the conditions which will ensure the formation of an action with predetermined properties. In respect to habits, for example, this would mean something quite different from the usual practice of finding the "trial and error" curve, that is to say, the selection of such conditions that would eliminate errors and confidently bring forth the pre-determined properties of the action. The difference between these two research procedures is that, in the first case, we only state the result — gradual reduction of errors — without being able to clarify, as a rule, neither the "intervening" central activity on which directly, primarily and largely depends execution, nor the conditions — all the conditions — which make this very activity possible. In the second case, every error is treated as a task of finding a sign which will enable the individual to avoid this error. Such putting up of signs goes on until they provide the individual (who has the necessary previous knowledge and ability, but is unable to carry out a new action) with the possibility of performing the new action correctly from the first try and every next time onwards.
42	
43	These investigations yielded the following conclusions. 1 ) Simultaneously with actions, sensory images and concepts of the objects of these actions are formed, *i.e.*, the formation of actions, images and concepts represent the different sides of the same process. Moreover, the action and object patterns are, to a considerable extent, interchangeable in the sense that certain properties of an object begin to denote definite modes of action, while every link of an action is connected with definite properties of its object. 2 ) The mental plane is only one of the planes of ideation, exactly as the level (or plane) of perception. The plane of speech can represent the third independent plane in the activity of an individual. At any rate, the mental plane is formed only on the basis of the speech form of action. 3 ) Action is transferred to the ideational plane either wholly or in its orientation part. In this latter case, the execution part of action remains in the material plane and, changing together with the orientation part, turns at end into a motor habit. 4 ) The transfer of action into the ideational plane, specially into the mental plane, is carried out through the reflection of its objective content by the means inherent to each of these planes and is expressed by repeated consecutive changes in the form of action. 5 ) The transfer of action into mental level (or plane), that is to say, its internalization, is only one of the lines along which it changes. Other unavoidable and no less important lines are represented by changes in the completeness of the action links, in the degree of their differentiation and of their assimilation, in the characteristics of rate, rythm and force. These changes cause, first of all, the succession of modes of execution and forms of reverse connection (feedback); then they determine the acquired properties of action. The former changes lead to the transformation of the mentally performed action into something that in self-observation appears to be a psychic process, while the latter enable to control the formation of such action properties as flexibility, reasoning, conscientiousness, critical attitudes, etc.
44	
45	## THREE BASIC TYPES OF LEARNING
46	
47	After the investigations of W. Kohler ( 1917 ), we can no longer doubt that reasoning in action has completely objective features. Its measure is found in how much the child is orienting the action by its substantial, external, objective conditions. Naturally, we wished to ensure reasoning in action as much as possible and we came, one day, so near to the solution of this problem that we obtained an altogether new type of process. The trials and errors, so characteristic of the traditional formation of actions, have become rare and incidental, the duration of formation has been sharply curtailed (at the expense of most protracted and difficult period of establishing the right action), the deviations of quality in separate executions have become insignificant; transfer has brown substantially, even the attitude of the individual towards the process of learning has changed. The new way of forming actions and concepts was named Type II, in order to mark its difference from the usual, commonly known Type I which historically came first. A comparison between Type I and Type II conditions will easily explain, at the same time, the mechanisms of Type I and its hitherto predominance in learning : the incompleteness of conditions required for the correct performance of a new assignment makes it unavoidable and even advantageous in a way. At the same time, another type of instruction, Type III, was discovered; some of its details will be discussed later.
48	
49	This is how the theory of the three basic types of mental action and concept formation was elaborated. Its most general assumption is that the type of orientation in a matter, the process of learning, the quality of its results, the attitude toward the matter and the process of learning are closely intertwined and form an unitary process, the process of learning. The decisive part in this process is the type of orientation in the matter; that is why we say "type of orientation" for meaning the corresponding type of learning and "type of learning" for meaning the corresponding type of orientation. The theory of basic types of learning is so important for the "learning and development" problem that we feel justified to discuss it at some length. To simplify the explanation, let us introduce some notions (Galperin, 1966 ).
50	
51	The totality of conditions by which the individual is *actually* guided in performing the action will be called the Orienting Basis of the Action ($Oa$). The system of conditions which are to be taken into account if we want that the new action should performed correctly, "from the first try and every next time onwards", will be called the System of Conditions for Correct Performance ($Sp$). $Oa$ may be equal to, or less than $Sp$ ($Oa \leq Sp$). If $Oa > Sp$, that is to say, if $Oa$ contains extra, or may be even false signs, this will to some extent interfere with the correct execution of the action; functionally $Oa$ will be, in this instance, less than $Sp$. Here, in relationship of $Oa$ with $Sp$, "more" will every time mean "less".
52	
53	So, $Oa \leq Sp$. If $Oa < Sp$, we have the formation of Type I; if $Oa = Sp$, we have the Type II. Let us consider these two cases.
54	
55	***Type I :*** $Oa < Sp$. This means that the individual lacks some of the signs that are essential for the correct performance of a new action; he is unable to reconstruct them without help; if he well carries out such a reconstruction, the formation turns out into Type II. There where the signs are missing, the individual has only got one way open to him, that of blind trials. In this case, errors are unavoidable, and yet incidental success is possible. These successful actions, however, will be noticed only for their result, for often they are far from the best and, what is more important, they will not always lead to the discovery of their objective conditions. That is why successful actions, purposeful as they are, remain, for most part, unreasoned. Correct actions are formed slowly; the process is clearly divided into two phases of unequal duration : the phase in which the general structure of the action is established and the phase in which the action is perfected. In the long run, the action may reach its utmost precision, limited only by physiological possibilities, but as much as a considerable part of its objective conditions remains unknown to the individual, it is highly subject to changes with the even unimportant changes in conditions. This is the origin of fluctuations of quality that are permanently observed in individual performance and out of proportion with the changes in conditions and that also the image, as it were, of spontaneous fluctuations in the characteristics of individual executions. The degree of success in learning greatly varies among the individuals. A transfer to new assignments is very unsatisfactory in a majority of individuals. Since separate executions and the formation of action on the whole are mainly controlled by the final result, the attitude of the individual towards the process of learning is determined by his attitude towards this result and is of purely utilitarian nature.
56	
57	***Type II :*** $Oa = Sp$. In this case, the individual has a complete system of signs at his disposal to perform correctly a new assignment which he follows without failure; otherwise, the return to Type I occurs. Thus the first, longest and most difficult period of acquisition of the action becomes unnecessary. This means that the process of learning is already curtailed considerably. In Type II, every link of the action is correlated to its objective condition, and *in this measure* the action is not only successful, but also reasoned. Very soon it reaches the same physiological possibilities, but now the characteristics of separate performances are far more stable, because the objective signs of the action are very prominent among all other environmental components. The degree of success in learning among the individuals becomes more uniform; group success shows greater uniformity at a higher level. Transfer grows considerably, compared to that in Type I. It shows much fluctuation in different assignments, but even at its best it remains by far incomplete. Since the conditions of the correct performance are given to the individual, his interest is growing into a successful and ever more perfect execution of the assignment, in the very process of learning. This is precisely an interest in the success of his action only, as his attitude towards its result and towards learning in general remains utilitarian. But the final result loses its importance as the only criterion, while the apparent significance of singling out the conditions of action and therefore, of theoretical research, is met with increasing recognition.
58	
59	The advantages of the Type II, as compared to Type I are quite evident and important, especially when the task is limited to mastering separate assignments. But at school, learning assignments usually refer to a certain sphere and come in a long succession. In respect for such a succession, the basis short-coming of Type II learning becomes clearly indicated : the $Oa$ for *every* new assignment has to be indicated from the beginning (transfer remains substantially incomplete); it is to be found empirically, by finding out how much the suggested signs will eliminate errors made by the individuals. This shortcoming of Type II led to the new task of teaching the child how to construct a complete $Oa$ for new assignments, at least for a definite and limited sphere. Evidently, the desired result will be obtained when the child is provided with such a method of analysis to construct, without assistance, a complete $Oa$ for any phenomenon in this sphere. It is also clear that such an analysis must bear on — ( 1 ) the "basic units" of the material of the sphere in question and — ( 2 ) the general rules of their combination in concrete phenomena. In accordance with this, when faced with the first objects from a new sphere, the individual acquires two methods : the method of singling out the basic units from the concrete objects and the method of caracterizing their combinations in these objects. The results is a complete $Oa$ of the object at hand which is different from the empirically constructed complete $Oa$ of Type II, by its rational origin. Such a complete and rational $Oa$ becomes the basis for the acquisition of actions and concepts related to the studied object.
60	
61	## LEARNING BY THE METHOD OF TYPE III
62	
63	The kind of instruction which we called Type III was successfully implemented at first in teaching writing of letters and words. Even in comparison with Type II, the results were proved to be so much better in all respects that we decided to generalize it in applying this type of instruction to the considerably different material : grammatical analysis of words, initial arithmetical operations and concepts.
64	
65	### *Writing of letters*
66	
67	In teaching writing of letters, the contour was chosen as the object to be studied and mastered, or, to be more exact, its shape, size and localization (Galperin, 1958 ). We took the "line of unchanging direction" as unit of contour : at points where the line or its direction began to change, division dots were put on so that the line was located between two such dots. The localization of the dots themselves was indicated at the crosspoints of the "slanting" line with the "reclining" one (the vertical and horizontal lines of the page). Six-year-old children were taught to find the division dots, to supply them with *verbal* characteristics (by describing their localization on the network of coordinates in the page), to transfer the key dots, in accordance with these verbal characteristics, to the clean space on the line and then, following the dots, to reproduce the contour. All these actions requiring orientation in the structure and localization of the contour were transferred by stages to the mental plane where they were brought up to the level of automatism. After that, the children were writing letters and words without thinking of how they had done it. After such a learning for the first few letters, the children mastered independently the writing of the rest of the letters of the Russian alphabet (totalizing 58 letters, capital letters included). During control tests, they had no difficulty in analyzing and reproducing latin letters, Armenian and Arabic writing, shorthand characters, drawings, sketches; they could trace trajectories described by only moving bodies on a plane. Due to the clear arrangement of objects in space, they could count things much better. In a word, transfer was not only complete in the first selected sphere (writing of Russian letters) but reached far beyond its borders covering all the assignments where it was important to take into account the arrangement of objects on a plane. The method of teaching is itself noteworthy. The child was asked not only to show, but also to prove that the line changes at this point exactly and not at an adjacent one, to mark the place of a key dot by drawing auxiliary lines if necessary, and to describe verbally in such a way that another child could indicate its exact localization, etc. In all instances, we resorted to the judgement of other children and asked the individual to describe each element so that it would have the same meaning for all. In other words, the children were systematically taught to develop an objective awareness of the plane structure and of the characteristics of its elements, and to use this knowledge for the recognition and reproduction of concrete graphical objects.
68	
69	### *Grammar*
70	
71	The study of the grammar of the Russian language (the mother tongue) in accordance with Type III begins with a separate word (Zhdan, 1968 ); in the Russian language, it carries many of the meanings which, in other languages, are communicated by articles, prepositions and the position of the word in a sentence. For "basic units" of the word, we took semes (not morphemes) — the smallest, further indivisible "units of information" concerning gender, number, 'case, degree, person, etc. Often several such semes are found in one morpheme which can even be zero, and sometimes the same seme passes from one morpheme into the other. These units of information were singled out by changing systematically certain parts of the word and comparing (in respect to sound composition and meaning) the initial one and the modified form of the word which were written one under the other. The discovered meanings were written down nearby with arrows pointing to them from the respective parts of the word.
72	
73	This analysis, carried out on different words from different parts of speech, yields a general scheme of the word, and of all the words, which is a common carrier of a somewhat intricate, but clearly outlined system of possible combinations for different units of information. This scheme becomes an instrument of speech activity, while speech itself turns into an active process of using different semes for the desired presentation of things. In this meaning, language becomes indeed not only an instrument of activity, but activity itself (it is Humboldt's idea, though without Humboldt's mysticism). It is noteworthy that this way of understanding the language, *i.e.*, from the aspect of people's consciousness fixed in it, "language consciousness", simplifies considerably the task of characterizing language phenomena and makes it possible to present them with more regularity. This, in its turn, provides an opportunity to ensure a correct orientation among these phenomena and their mastering by means achieving grammatical tasks. This way of correct grammar is directly applied to written and oral speech, without satiety and with ever increasing interest. The initial and basic principle of discrimination of the smallest units of information leads in an easily understood way to a quick and assured formation of the "feeling of language" which can be extensively transferred to the learning of foreign languages, the study of literature and precise formulation of logical thought. This way of learning a language is not merely acquisition of the rules of grammatically correct speech, but also discovery of a new world, the world of people's consciousness concerning things, people and their relations. It is, at the same time, a new structure of speech thinking supported by the basic patterns which have been deposited in this language consciousness.
74	
75	## THE IIId TYPE IN LEARNING OF FIRST MATHEMATICAL CONCEPTS
76	
77	The application of the Type III of orientation to the initial primary, learning of numbers and arithmetical actions deserves a more detailed discussion as this we had when facing the problems of "learning and development" (Galperin, 1959, 1960 ). The key point in primary arithmetics is the concept of unity because it is the basis on which all other numbers and numerical operations are constructed. For a long time, we have been using the traditional definition of unity as separate thing, but now the requirement of rationality (justification of an action by the properties of its object) led us to revise this question radically.
78	
79	For the person who begins to be acquainted with arithmetics, a number always denotes the result of measuring¹. Consequently, the learning of arithmetics must start with measuring. So we began by demonstrating to six-year-old children the importance of measuring for various spheres of life : we took them on shopping tours where they watched how foodstuffs were weighed, shoes, hats and clothes tried on, fabrics measured, etc. Back to school, we told them : "You see, measuring is very used everywhere; let us start measuring too. What are we going to measure ? Well, everything : tables, windows, doors, anything... What shall we measure with ? It depends on what we want to measure. If we want to measure length, we must use something long, if we want to measure water or sand we must use a spoon, a cup, a bucket (*i.e.*, something that has capacity) and if we want to measure weight, we must use something heavy as a weight. We cannot measure water (capacity) by using a string (length), or weight, by using a cup or a spoon. In a word, every property of a thing can be measured only with its own measure. It is why it is very important to understand which of the properties of the thing has to be measured, which one is asked about, as the same thing has various properties". The ability to point
80	
81	¹ We were happy to learn afterwards that the Academician Henri Lebesgue ( 1938 ) thought it was possible to build basic kinds of numbers by measuring and that the Academician A.N. Kolmorogorov stressed, in his foreword to the Russian translation of Lebesgue's book, that measuring is the only strictly scientific method of obtaining numbers in the process of instruction.
82	
83	out the property in question (what is of which one is there more, less or equal quantity ?) was the matter of careful learning. At the same time, children were taught to carry out the measuring procedure. At first, they handled the measure carelessly : now it was undersized, then it was oversized, sometimes there were gaps between two measures, sometimes the measured parts overlapped. Appealing to the judgement of other children, we pointed out these irregulatiries and showed that the result was also incorrect.
84	
85	Going on with divisible things, we suggested to "measure" objects which could neither be divided nor even marked out (not to spoil the thing) : the length of a table, a window-still, or a door. Water in a bucket was measured with cups which were emptied into a big pan, thus the separateness of the measured quantities disappears. At first the children were carried away by the measuring procedure to such an extent that they failed to notice a material result. When asked how much it makes at the end, they were quite at a loss. Then the experimenter (E.) asked again; "What shall we do to find out how much it makes ?" Sometimes the children themselves were able to indicate the method and sometimes it was prompted by the E. : every measure applied to the thing would be marked with something; the use of various small objects was suggested for the purpose. The accumulated quantity of marks is equal to the number of measures applied. After each time the measure was applied, the E. asked : "What does this button, or this match stand for ? And what do theses bits stand for ?". The child answered (and if he made a mistake, other children corrected him) that each of the "things" stood for one measure while all the bits stood for the number of times the measure was applied. In this way, a thing was represented as a multitude. All these actions were carried on to a correct and quick performance, and the children acquired the ability — ( 1 ) to distinguish the property in question, to single it out among all other properties of the thing, and — ( 2 ) to represent the thing in accordance with this property as a multiplicity of "applied measures".
86	
87	Then the children were taught to compare the multiplicities they had obtained. They were offered disorderly two rather large groups of objects (of 15 to 20 pieces each) used as marks and that differed in their number only by 1 or 2 pieces (visually this difference could not be established, and the children did not know how to count). The E. asked whether these groups were the same or any of the groups was larger than the other. At first, the children answered at random and, of course, the answers were different. But the E. asked : "How can we prove to all who is right" ? If the children found difficult to answer the question, the E. showed them the method of two mutually correlated (horizontal) rows. This became the chief method of the quantitative comparison of multiplicities and, from them too, of values. Such an arrangement of rows easily led to the formation of concepts like "the same quantity, equal", "more - less", "more by" (and "less by") "so many" elements. Only after such a preliminary learning, we introduced the first concrete number, the unity. It was given the definition of something that was measured and was equal to its measure. Here we stressed the relation of the unity's equality with its own (and only its own) measure : if a different measure was used, the former unity was no longer unity. The following three numbers, 0, 2, 3, were constructed by the E. in accordance with the latent rule $n \pm 1$, after which the rule itself was introduced as a double rule : every following number is greater by 1, every number preceding the one which is indicated is less by 1. Following theses rules, the children by themselves constructed new multiplicities (numbers), while the E. told them how these numbers were called and written. The children did not know yet how to write and used the figures that were printed on cards. Then they performed various actions with every emerging number.
88	
89	This scheme was used by L.S. Georgiyev ( 1960 ) in working out a detailed method. While keeping this method, he has been carrying on the study of numbers and the first four number actions within the first ten in a kindergarten. The experiment covered the whole "school year" ( 8 months), a pre-school programme. Before this experiment was carried out, we had wanted for some time to demonstrate the advantage of this method over the traditional practices of teaching and then Georgiyev had the good idea of using Piaget's problems for this purpose. Preliminary tests revealed a striking fact : the children who had been trained with the traditional method and counted well (far beyond 10 ), did not resort at all to counting when they were brought face to face with Piaget's problems, giving answers on the basis of "apparent" relations. Then the main experiment was conducted in the following way : at the beginning of a new school year, 50 children from senior groups of three different nursery schools, with well-organized (traditional) instruction in arithmetics, received 16 Piaget's problems (original or constructed by Georgiyev from Piaget); then they were re-taught with our method and, finally, after 8 months², they were given the same problems again. For comparison, the same problems at the same time were presented to 60 "graduates" of control groups who had been taught by the traditional method and had got good and excellent marks in arithmetics. The results produced the Table 1.

104	
105	In this way, the formation of primary mathematical concepts achieved with the Type III of orientation brought the children, after a short span of time, to the end of the period of intellectual development that the control subjects had barely entered. As far as we are concerned, this result had led us directly to the problem of intellectual development in one of its most important stage, which is transitional from pre-scientific thinking to first really scientific one (or, to use Piaget's terminology, from the pre-operational stage to the stage of concrete operations). Of course, the changes that are taking place, are not limited to thinking, but at the present moment we have to accept this limitation.
106	
107	## CHANGES IN INTELLECTUAL DEVELOPMENT
108	
109	We have been bringing forth these changes ourselves planning them and controlling them sufficiently so that we can plainly see all their successive stages. Theses changes appear to us in the following manner.
110	
111	Before our teaching, all the parameters of a thing were equivalent for the child. Every time he compared objects in respect to the property which seemed to emerge "by itself", considering it as a characteristic of the whole thing and, in consequence, of all its properties. After our teaching, the thing, in the child's mind, became divided into separate and relatively independent properties, so that he was then comparing things only in respect to one of the properties, the one he has been asked about. In a word, previously the whole thing was a value, whereas now each thing has become a collection of many different values. Therefore now the first and main question for the child is this : "Which of the values is in question and which of the values actually changes, the one I am asked about or a different one ?" This distinction was of primary importance, because all Piaget's problems were constructed in such a way that it was sufficient to separate these parameters in order to understand, for example, that only the level of liquid was changed, but not its volume, the shape of a plasticine ball but not the quantity of material, the length of a row in space but not the number of its elements, the mutual localization of the ends of two blocks of wood but not their length, etc. Under the surface of things, the child began to visualize their internal structure where each property had a separate value, the magnitude of which was found not by directly comparing one object to the other but by breaking each value into equivalent elements and subsequently correlating these multiplicities or their numerical meanings. Moreover even a direct comparison of two objects suggested that one of the values was accepted for a measure and, as it was equal to this measure, made a unity.
112	
113	The second change is that, after its division into elements equal to the measure, a value (a separate property of thing) becomes a multiplicity. From this time onwards, the compared objects, instead of remaining a sensory variety of things in which their mathematical multiplicity lies deeply under the surface, become a real mathematical multiplicity (revealed within one property !), the elements of which are similar in respect to a definite feature represented by the measure. The "kick" of our teaching method is that it introduces no contradiction with visual perception (this contradiction is exactly what Piaget so brillantly uses in his problems, where its use is justified by the purpose which is the reverse of our own : not to form, but simply reveal certain structures of thinking); instead of that, being still within the borders of visual perception, it develops a new approach to things which leaves out the domain of the naively egocentrical images.
114	
115	Finally, the third change is that these multiplicities receive a general internal organization in a rule (the formation of natural numbers) and in their arrangement in a definite (decimal) system. This is the basis upon which the "principle of conservation of quantity" is also developed. First of all, it appears as a fact, the fact that if "we have neither added nor withdrawn anything", the changing of a thing in one of its properties (*e.g.*, the level of water in a vessel, the shape of a plasticine ball, the length of a row of things in space) does not involve any change in its other properties (the quantity of water, of plasticine, or the number of bits). The compensation of such a change by reversely changing something else (*e.g.*, the compensation of height by width, of length by thickness, etc.) that Piaget considers as the basis of child's decision about "conservation of quantity", is an auxiliary explanation which is possible only when the different parameters of a thing are exactly connected, *e.g.*, a considerable change of length and the correspondingly considerable change of width and thickness (the level of water and its volume, the length and the thickness of the plasticine roll); but the number of bits and the length of the row, for example, are quite unconnected, and the above explanation is therefore unsuitable.
116	
117	The division of a thing into different values, the presentation of each value as a multiplicity and the determination of the general structure of numbers (of these multiplicities) are the three interconnected patterns through which the children now see the empirical objects and which they use for carrying out operations in evaluating their quantitative relations. Throughout the experimental teaching, we clearly observed that the relationship between the direct sensory image of things and the mental pattern was undergoing a change. At first, the children "taught by bitter experience", simply avoided giving a straighforward judgement about values : "Let's measure, let's measure !" they said before venturing an answer to a question in Piaget's problem. Then, when differenciation of parameters become sufficiently stable, there came a phase where the two points of view co-existed : the children saw the quantitative relations of things according to Piaget, but they realized that actually these relations were different. They could express this duality very well : "It seems to be more (less), but really it is the same !" Perceptually the situation had not changed yet, while it was already different "in the mind" which penetrates beyond the surface of things.
118	
119	At about the same time, we observed an interesting fact already discovered by L.F. Obukhova ( 1966 ). In the middle of the period of learning (Type III), the children carried out the measuring of objects they had to compare only during the first phase of the experiment, when the objects were really equal and appeared equal to the eyes, whereas during the second phase of the experiment, after that one of the objects had been changed, they dispensed with measuring them. Why did they measure things when it was not needed, and why did not they measure things when it would have been useful ? From the point of view of objective results, such a behavior does not appear rational, but it is completely justified if it is considered psychologically (*i.e.*, as a means of strengthening the still shaky idea of the separate basic properties of a thing and of the parameter by which the comparison is to be carried out), if this perceptually weak parameter in perception has to be supported and singled out from the general, global impression which is still dominant in the perceptual field. This middle stage of co-existence, if we may speak so, of a direct perception and of the new mental plane is followed by the final stage, in our experimental teaching, in which the mental plane becomes prevailing over the visual image. The children no longer feel any contradiction between what "seems" and what "really is". The thing which changes in the second phase of Piaget's experiment is immediately limited to one parameter which is clearly distinguished by the children from the parameter of the question : the level of water in the vessel has changed, but not the quantity of water; the distance between the bits has changed, but not their number, and so on.
120	
121	## THE CONNECTION BETWEEN LEARNING AND INTELLECTUAL DEVELOPMENT
122	
123	Thus, the Type III of learning leads to three fundamental changes in the image of things : instead of "just a thing", we now have a complex of relatively independent parameters; instead of "just a property", we have a multitude of "basic units"; instead of a disorderly multitude of parts, we have an organized system of units, the pattern of which is common to all the objects included in the studied sphere. Such a change of the naively egocentrical image does not take place only in mathematics. In the grammatical study of a word, it is also necessary at first to separate the lexical meaning of this word from its proper linguistic meaning; the latter is then presented as a multiplicity of semes, *i.e.* basic units of the language as a means of information, and, finally, a pattern of word is established which is common to all the words of any part of speech. In the calligraphic study of letters, the first thing to do is, so to say, to separate the topological aspect from the dimensional, properly graphical, content of the contour; then the graphical contour is presented as a multiplicity of pieces of line, whereas the coordinate network of the page forms a general pattern within which every contour is provided with its individual localization on the right basis line.
124	
125	The three patterns establish the rational structure of empirical objects. But they do not deal only with the structure of objects. At very stage of the process of emergence, these patterns serve as instruments in solving the problem related to objects under study. When he uses these instruments, the child moves along the lines of these patterns and learns how to follow them in different directions, thus mastering the patterns themselves. In this way, they become also patterns of thinking about things, *i.e.*, general patterns on the basis of which some theoretical actions are undertaken and achieved, *i.e.*, new structures of thought. These structures condition the voluntary logical thinking : instead of blindly following the dominating stimulation, the subject follows a situationally weak but problematically important property; instead of seeing its quality with surprise, he transforms it into a quantity which opens a way to action; instead of being stopped by a disorderly multitude, he organizes it according to a general pattern which allows him to find ways to the solution of a problem.
126	
127	If the Type III of orientation is followed, concrete knowledge about facts and laws of the sphere under study, is formed on the basis of these general patterns : in arithmetics, the knowledge of concrete numbers and of actions performed with them; in morphology, the separate groups and categories of words and the rules according to which they change and agree; in calligraphy, the peculiarities of individual letters, the way by which they are written separately or combined with other letters, and so on. But such a relationship between concrete knowledge and general patterns is established only in the Type III of learning. In the other types of learning (Type I and even Type II), concrete knowledge about facts and laws is assimilated without any connection with these general patterns and, for the most part, without any idea about them. This is why, outside the Type III of learning, the knowledge of facts and even laws does not directly influence the development of thought. Of course, these general patterns are present, but in a hidden manner, in the knowledge acquired through the Type I and Type II. But in this condition, the practical application of knowledge can result in a spontaneous, "intuitional" approximation to these general patterns and in the correspondingly spontaneous reorganization of thought. But it occurs for different individuals with varying degree of success, and even when thought begins to follow these patterns in some degree, they are still treated in a subconscious manner and remain within the framework of the empirical material from which they emerged.
128	
129	Thus all acquirements of learning can be divided into two unequal parts. The first one consists of new general patterns of things which condition a new way of seeing things and thinking about them; the other one consists of concrete facts and laws found in the sphere under study, that is to say, the concrete material of a science. In its totality, the second part has more weight than the first one, but in the same measure its importance is inferior to the first one as for development of thought. Only the Type III of orientation in the subject leads to clearly understand the relationship between the general patterns of things with scientific knowledge of them and, at the same time, the relationship between learning and development of thought, for only with this type of teaching, the programme of instruction includes the formation of generalized patterns of reality which, by study, become the generalized and unified patterns of individual actions and new structures of thought. It is possible therefore, that the Type III is the kind of learning, theorically admitted by Piaget, in which the acquisition of knowledge goes side by side with intellectual development, though the two process remain, of course, the different sides of the same process. This type of learning, however, has been hitherto unknown, whereas with all other types of learning, there is a wide gap between the acquisition of knowledge and the development of though, so that the relationship between them becomes so remote and vague that it allows a great variety of interpretations. Hence it is clear that in the study of intellectual development, the description of separate stages, even if this description is made by such a penetrating master of psychological analysis as Piaget, states merely the achieved conditions and cannot give sufficient basis to judge of process of development or dynamic forces. At the present time, only the organization of the stage-by-stage formation of mental actions and concepts (characteristic of the period of development upon which our interest is centered), through the use of Type III of orientation in the subject, does open a way to studying intellectual development in its dynamics, in the operation of its motivational forces and its emerging mechanisms.
130
